mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Finland on?
mapQuestions.answer: Europe, in the far north (Nordic region). Finland borders Sweden, Norway and Russia, with the Baltic Sea to the south 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Finland?
mapQuestions.answer: Helsinki, on the south coast. About 660,000 people. Quiet, sea-edged, with elegant Russian-influenced 19th-century architecture mixed with modernist designs.

mapQuestions.question: What language does Finland speak?
mapQuestions.answer: Finnish and Swedish are both official. Finnish is unlike most European languages, related instead to Hungarian and Estonian. Most younger Finns also speak excellent English.

mapQuestions.question: Where does Santa Claus live?
mapQuestions.answer: In Finnish Lapland! The official "Santa Claus Village" is just north of Rovaniemi, exactly on the Arctic Circle. Children write to Santa here from all over the world, and the post office stamps every letter with a special Arctic mark.

mapQuestions.question: How many lakes does Finland have?
mapQuestions.answer: About 188,000! Finland is often called "the land of a thousand lakes" but the real number is far more. Around 10% of the country's surface is fresh water.

foods.name: Karjalanpiirakka
foods.description: Karelian pies: rye-flour pasty boats filled with rice porridge, served warm with butter-egg "munavoi" spread on top. The Finnish snack of choice.

foods.name: Lohikeitto
foods.description: A creamy salmon-and-potato soup with dill, leek and butter. The Finnish winter dish for warming up after a snowy day outside.

foods.name: Ruisleipä
foods.description: Dark, dense, sour rye bread baked into round flat loaves with a hole in the middle (traditionally for hanging from rafters). The Finnish staple.

foods.name: Pulla
foods.description: Sweet cardamom-spiced bread, often braided. Served at every Finnish coffee break ("kahvitauko"), which is a national institution.

foods.name: Reindeer stew
foods.description: Slow-cooked reindeer with mashed potatoes, lingonberry jam, and pickled cucumber. The Lapland classic.

foods.name: Wild forest berries
foods.description: Bilberries, lingonberries, cloudberries and arctic raspberries, picked wild from Finnish forests in late summer. Finland's "everyman's right" lets anyone pick them on any land.

scavengerItems.label: A traditional wooden sauna (Finland has more saunas than cars)
Santa Claus Village just outside Rovaniemi in Lapland
The aurora borealis (Northern Lights) glowing green over a frozen lake
A reindeer crossing a country road (Lapland)
A husky sled team running across frozen forest
Helsinki Cathedral (white with green domes on a Senate Square hill)
A pine or birch forest stretching to the horizon
A glassy lake in summer, with a wooden sauna cottage on the shore
Finns drinking coffee (Finland drinks more coffee per person than any country on Earth)
A snow castle or ice hotel in Kemi (winter only)
Wood-fired sauna smoke rising from a small island
A swimming jump from a sauna pier into a freezing lake

geoMatches.description: The capital, on the Baltic Sea. Elegant Senate Square, the white-and-green Cathedral, Suomenlinna sea fortress, and modernist design everywhere (Alvar Aalto, Marimekko).
The Lapland capital, just on the Arctic Circle. Home to Santa Claus Village, the Arktikum museum, and the start of Lapland reindeer and husky adventures.
The huge lake district in central Finland, with 188,000 lakes, islands, and forest. Finns spend their summers here in wooden lake cottages ("mökki").
A famous Lapland resort with glass igloos so you can watch the Northern Lights from your bed in winter.
The oldest city in Finland (founded 1229), Finland's former capital, with a riverside castle and the Aboa Vetus archaeology museum.
A 1748 sea fortress on islands off Helsinki harbour, accessible by a 15-minute ferry. UNESCO World Heritage.

senses.smell: Pine and birch smoke from a sauna stove, fresh rye bread from a Finnish bakery, cold clean Arctic air, cardamom-rich pulla coffee buns, fresh blueberries crushed underfoot in late summer…
senses.hear: The crackle of a sauna stove, distant church bells across a lake, "Hei!" greetings everywhere, the deep silence of a snow-covered forest, the soft creak of cross-country skis on packed snow…
senses.taste: Buttery Karelian pies, sweet cloudberry jam, salty rye bread with cheese, hot creamy salmon soup, cardamom-warm pulla on a coffee break…
senses.feel: The shock of jumping from a 90°C sauna into a freezing lake, soft snow under boots, the cosy warmth of a lake-cottage fire, dry rye crispbread snapping in your fingers, the cold of a glass igloo wall…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why are Finns obsessed with saunas?
stories.body: There are roughly 3.3 MILLION saunas in Finland, in a country of 5.5 million people. That is more saunas than cars. Almost every Finnish home, summer cottage, school, even some workplaces, has one. The Finnish sauna is hot (80 to 100°C), bone-dry, and usually wood-fired. You throw water on the hot stones ("löyly") to make steam, sit and sweat for 10 to 15 minutes, then run out and jump into a freezing lake (in summer) or roll in the snow (in winter), then go back into the sauna. UNESCO put Finnish sauna culture on its "intangible cultural heritage" list in 2020. Saunas are where Finns relax, gossip, and even hold business meetings. The Finnish word "sauna" is now in every language on Earth.

stories.question: Is Santa Claus really Finnish?
stories.body: For decades, Finnish Lapland has been the official home of Santa Claus. The "Santa Claus Village", just outside Rovaniemi exactly on the Arctic Circle, is open all year. Children visit Santa's log-cabin office, post letters at the special Santa Claus Post Office (postmarked with an Arctic Circle stamp), feed reindeer, ride huskies, and sometimes see the Northern Lights overhead. Around half a million children write to the address "Santa Claus Main Post Office, Santa's Village, FI-96930 Arctic Circle, Finland" every year. Each letter gets an answer back. Lapland's short winter days (in December the sun barely rises) and snowy forests make it look exactly like every Christmas card you have ever seen.
stories.thinkingQuestion: A whole part of the world has decided to be Santa's home. What other places have built themselves around a story like that?

stories.question: What is "sisu"?
stories.body: "Sisu" (pronounced SEE-soo) is a Finnish word that has no direct translation in English. It means something like quiet strength, determination, calm grit when things get hard. Finnish stories say that "sisu" is what kept Finland going through brutal winters, through two wars against the Soviet Union in the 1940s, and through years of slow rebuilding afterwards. It is not loud. It is not boastful. It is just keeping going when most people would have stopped. Modern Finns still talk about sisu often. Marathon runners say it. Soldiers say it. Saunagoers in -25°C winters say it. It is one of the most loved words in the Finnish language.

stories.question: Why are there so many lakes?
stories.body: Finland has about 188,000 lakes (the Finnish Environment Institute confirms 187,888 with surface area greater than 500 square metres). They are leftovers from the last Ice Age, around 12,000 years ago, when a giant ice sheet covered Finland. As the ice retreated, it gouged out hollows in the land that filled with melted water, leaving the modern Finnish lake-and-forest landscape. Lake Saimaa, the biggest, has 13,710 islands inside it. Most Finns own or have access to a "mökki" (a wooden lake cottage with a sauna), and summer holidays are spent there, swimming, fishing, picking berries, doing not much.

stories.question: How does the sun behave near the Arctic Circle?
stories.body: In Lapland, the sun does not work normally. Around midsummer (21 June), the sun does NOT SET for several weeks. This is "yötön yö", the "nightless night", or midnight sun. Children play football outdoors at 2am in broad daylight. Around midwinter (21 December), the opposite: the sun does not RISE for several weeks. This is "kaamos", the polar night. Lapland is lit by the moon, the stars, the Northern Lights, and the soft blue twilight that the locals call "the blue moment". Finns deal with both extremes by drinking enormous amounts of coffee (Finland drinks the most coffee per person of any country on Earth), and by trusting that the seasons will keep turning.

convoQuestions.question: Best food in Finland so far?
Have you been in a sauna yet? How long did you last?
Helsinki, Lapland, lakes, archipelago, which has felt the most "Finland"?
Did you see the Northern Lights, or are you still hoping?
Finnish "sisu" is a quiet kind of grit. What do you call that in your family?

wordSearch.hint: Finnish foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Reindeer
animals.description: About 200,000 semi-domesticated reindeer roam Finnish Lapland. Each is owned by a Sámi family. Often crossing roads in winter.

animals.name: Brown bear
animals.description: Finland's national animal. Around 2,000 bears live in eastern Finnish forests. You can join guided bear-hide watches near the Russian border in summer.

animals.name: Whooper swan
animals.description: The national bird, on the 1-euro coin. Big white swans with golden bills, on Finnish lakes from spring to autumn.

animals.name: Grey wolf
animals.description: Around 250 wolves in Finnish forests, mostly in the east. Hard to spot but their tracks turn up in winter snow.

animals.name: Ural owl
animals.description: A big grey-brown forest owl, common across Finland. Calls echo through the spruce forests at dusk.

animals.name: European beaver
animals.description: Reintroduced to Finnish rivers and now thriving. Look for gnawed willow stumps along lake shores.

animals.name: Arctic fox
animals.description: Critically endangered in Finland (fewer than 10 wild ones). White in winter, brown in summer. Mostly in the far north.
